did.unittitle: Jonathan Edwards collection

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract:
The collection consists of writings, correspondence, documents, printed materials, photographs, and artifacts documenting the lives and work of Jonathan Edwards and his family. Jonathan Edwards' papers contain the great majority of his surviving manuscripts. These include over one thousand sermons; private theological and philosophical notebooks, including the nine volume "Miscellanies" and four volume "Notes on the Scripture;" Edwards'interleaved Bible. In addition, there is a box of Jonathan Edwards' correspondence; principal correspondents include Joseph Bellamy and Thomas Foxcroft.
Other Edwards family papers include several of Timothy Edwards's sermon notebooks; correspondence belonging to Jonathan Edwards (1745-1801), Jonathan Walter Edwards, and Hannah Edwards Wetmore (principal correspondents are Calvin Chapin, Moses Tryon, Joseph Bellamy, Lucy Wetmore Whittelsey and Chauncey Whittelsey); printed material (chiefly numerous family Bibles) and a broadside Thanksgiving Proclamation by George Washington dated October 1789; photographs of Edwards family members; and papers relating to the history of the collection.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: The bulk of the Jonathan Edwards Collection came to Yale as a gift of the heirs of Jonathan Edwards in 1900. Additional individual pieces, small groups of his papers, and especially, other family papers, have been added over the years through gift and purchase, chiefly from other Edwards descendants. For further acquisitions information, please consult the appropriate curator.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Jonathan Edwards Collection is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Jonathan Edwards Collection. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

relatedmaterial.head: Associated Materials
relatedmaterial.p: The Andover Newton Theological Seminary Collection of Jonathan Edwards (GEN MSS 1542), Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University, is a small yet significant collection of Edwards manuscripts, including a large number of family letters, several of Edwards's early "scientific" essays, and Book 6 of the "Miscellanies," containing entries 1067 and 1068, relating to the prophecies of the Messiah.

Though the bulk of Edwards's manuscripts are at Yale, other institutions do possess important pieces. For example, the manuscript of the "Spider Letter" is at the New-York Historical Society. Certain manuscripts, however, are not known to have survived. Among Edwards's autobiographical writings, for example, neither the "Diary" nor the "Personal Narrative" are thought to be extant. Though an index for the notes on "The Mind" has survived, the main text itself has not. For these writings, readers should consult printed editions of Edwards' works.

bioghist.head: JONATHAN EDWARDS (1703-1758)
bioghist.p: Jonathan Edwards, among the foremost theologians and philosophers of his time, was born in East Windsor, Connecticut, on October 5, 1703. He attended Yale College and graduated with a Bachelor of Arts in 1720. He returned in 1723 to receive his Master of Arts degree, and served as a tutor from 1724 to 1726. Upon leaving Yale, he succeeded his grandfather, Solomon Stoddard, as minister of the Congregationalist Church at Northampton, Massachusetts from 1726 to 1750. There he became known as an evangelical preacher and stern Calvinist, helping inspire the "Great Awakening" of the 1740s. His writings, which were widely circulated, included A Faithful Narrative , Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God , Distinguishing Marks of a Work of the Spirit of God , Some Thoughts Concerning the Revivals , and A Treatise Concerning Religious Affections .

Edwards eventually became alienated from his Northampton congregation, and was dismissed after a protracted controversy over qualifications for church membership. In 1751 he became a missionary to the Mahican and Mohawk Indians at Stockbridge, Massachusetts, and minister to the English congregation there. While at Stockbridge he continued writing treatises that expounded his theology, including Freedom of the Will , Original Sin , The Nature of True Virtue , and The End for Which God Created the World . In 1757, he accepted an offer to become the president of the College of New Jersey, now Princeton University. In March 1758 he received a smallpox innoculation which proved to be infected, and died.

For more information on Edwards's life, see Perry Miller, Jonathan Edwards (New York: Sloan, 1949); Ola E. Winslow, Jonathan Edwards, 1703-1758: A Biography (New York: Macmillan, 1941); and Iain H. Murray, Jonathan Edwards: A New Biography (Carlisle, Penn.: Banner of Truth, 1987). Readers should also consult The Works of Jonathan Edwards (New Haven: Yale University Press, 1957-ongoing) for further definitive information on Edwards's life and writings.

bioghist.head: JONATHAN EDWARDS FAMILY
bioghist.p: Note: The people listed here are represented prominently in the collection, and are therefore mentioned in the Description of the Collection.
Jonathan Walter m. Elizabeth Tryon (daughter of Moses Tryon)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Jonathan Edwards Collection consists of writings, correspondence, documents, printed materials, photographs, and artifacts documenting the lives and work of Jonathan Edwards and his family. The collection spans the years 1696-1972, but the bulk of the material dates from 1726-1758, during the years of Jonathan Edwards's ministry. The collection contains the great majority of Edwards's surviving manuscripts, and as such is a particularly rich resource for examining the man and his theology.

The Collection is housed in 38 boxes and is organized in ten series: Writings of Jonathan Edwards , Jonathan Edwards Correspondence , Church Papers , Edwards Family Writings , Edwards Family Correspondence , Genealogical Notes and Other Family Papers , Printed Material , Visual Material , Artifacts , and History of the Collection . Box 38 contains Oversize items.

Series I, Writings of Jonathan Edwards
(ca. 1720-1784), the largest series in the collection, is organized into three subseries: Sermons, Notebooks and Memoranda, and Personal Writings. There are over one thousand sermons, enabling the reader to track Edwards's homiletic activities over his entire career. The sermons are arranged in the order of the books of the Bible. A page number in parentheses refers to the page on which the doctrine or subject is found.

Edwards often wrote on odd pieces of paper that had been sewn together, and there is ample evidence of this habit in the Writings . These scraps may be anything from blank space on a letter he received, blank areas of printed items such as broadsides and newspapers, prayer requests from his congregation, and scraps from his daughters' painted paper fans (as described in Jane Greenfield's "Notable Bindings VIII," Yale University Gazette 68 [October 1993] 70-73). The sermons are an average 4" x 4", and the oversize sermons, housed in boxes 13 and 14 and also arranged in canonical order, are approximately 6" x 4". The sewing of these booklets indicates the order of construction, often giving clues to later revisions and additions.

Generally speaking, sermons written before 1742 are fully written out, but after that date they appear in outline form and are increasingly difficult to read. Edwards began to date his sermons in 1733, and it may be assumed that undated sermons which have the scripture text or the first page present were likely written before that date. It should be noted that until 1752 the Julian calendar was used, and dates on the sermons and correspondence in this collection reflect dating practices common at that time. Edwards had a practice of "reusing" his sermons, in some cases tipping in new parts of a sermon, in others simply bracketing parts of the sermon he wished to use. Those he altered carry multiple dates. Notes by The Works of Jonathan Edwards staff, used to identify and date many of the sermons, have been photocopied onto acid-free paper and are included with them.

Three sermon notebooks accompany the sermons, one of them containing a note for the Farewell Sermon Edwards gave at Northampton (box 14, folder 1154). There are also thirty-four folders of fragments, some with notes from others on the verso.

The sermons are supplemented by Edwards's private theological and philosophical notebooks and memoranda. These notebooks range from nearly finished versions of treatises to rough jottings. They are in a larger format than the sermons (mostly folio), and some are written in the margins of printed newspapers, broadsides, and books. A number of notebooks are dedicated to single themes, some of which are notes toward published materials. Included among the 41 notebooks are the nine volumes of "Miscellanies," eight volumes of theological notes composed of 1,360 entries of varying length, with an index created by Edwards; and four volumes of "Notes on the Scripture." There is also an interleaved Bible Edwards acquired from his brother-in-law, which is a disbound copy of the King James version of the Bible, interleaved with blank folio paper (box 17, folder 1216). There are detailed notes on every interleaved page relating to the text of the Bible opposite the page.

The Personal Writings section contains only five items, including a diary and memorandum book kept from 1733-1757 (box 21, folder 1267), and a copy of Edwards's will, dated March 1753, written in an unidentified hand (box 21, folder 1270). The original is in the Hampshire County Court House, Northampton, Massachusetts.

Series II, Jonathan Edwards Correspondence
(ca. 1735-1757), highlights Edwards's activities as a parent, pastor, and entrepreneur, his contacts with English and Scottish divines, and his efforts to publish such works as the Humble Inquiry and Freedom of the Will . The series is housed in one box and is organized into Outgoing Letters, Incoming Letters, and Third Party Correspondence. Principal correspondents include Joseph Bellamy and Thomas Foxcroft. Third Party Correspondence contains three letters written by others but concerning Jonathan Edwards. Series III, Church Papers
(1735-1751), is arranged chronologically and is housed in one box. It consists mostly of reports of meetings of the First Parish at Northampton. Included is the report of the council which voted the dismissal of Edwards in June 1750 (box 23, folder 1350).

The rest of the collection relates primarily to Jonathan Edwards's family and descendants, and to the history of the Jonathan Edwards papers prior to their arrival at Yale in 1900. Among these family members most frequently represented are Jonathan Edwards's father, Timothy Edwards (1669-1758), minister of East Windsor, Connecticut; son Jonathan Edwards (1745-1801) and his son Jonathan Walter Edwards; and sister Hannah Edwards Wetmore (1713-1773) and her daughter Lucy Wetmore Whittelsey.

Series IV, Edwards Family Writings
(1704-1904), is arranged alphabetically by author and then by title. There are a large number of religious writings such as sermons and confessions of faith, as well as personal diaries. Included are Timothy Edwards's sermon notebooks, the journal of Jonathan Edwards (1841-1886), kept while he was a child, four sermons by Chauncey Whittelsey, and a "memoranda" written by a Mrs. Pearce after the death of her husband, on a piece of paper joined to itself, which may have been worn as an armband (box 24, folder 1376). There are also fourteen sermons and other writings by unidentified authors.

Series V, Edwards Family Correspondence
(1715-1908) is organized into four subseries: Jonathan Edwards (1745-1801), Jonathan Walter Edwards, Hannah Edwards Wetmore, and Other Correspondents. In Jonathan Edwards's correspondence, there are seven folders of letters to his son Jonathan Walter Edwards, and two folders of letters from Calvin Chapin. Principal correspondents of Jonathan Walters Edwards include a Jonathan Edwards (whose letters date from 1814-1819), Moses Tryon, and Mary Edwards Hoyt. Hannah Edwards Wetmore's letters include letters from John Sargeant, a suitor of hers whose presence caused a rift with her betrothed, a Mr. Rockwell. A paper in Series X entitled, "Hard Hearted Hannah" by John De K. Alsop discusses this correspondence (box 37, folder 1669). The Other Correspondents file includes letters to Joseph Bellamy, Samuel Andrew Law, Chauncey Whittelsey, and Lucy Wetmore Whittelsey.

Series VI, Genealogical Notes and Other Family Papers
(1696-1891), is housed a single box. Besides the genealogical notes, it includes documents such as wills, indentures, deeds, certificates, a prayer in Mahican (box 29, folder 1596), and a bound volume of manuscript poems and printed illustrations belonging to Lydia Eliza Hollingsworth Kennedy (box 29, folder 1605).

Series VII, Printed Material
, is arranged alphabetically by title when known, or type of material; family Bibles and other volumes, however, are listed at the end of the series, as stored. The series includes pamphlets, ephemera removed from (and referenced to) family Bibles, clippings, an array of Bibles and religious works belonging to family members, and a broadside Thanksgiving Proclamation by George Washington in October 1789 (box 38, folder 1682).

Series VIII, Visual Material
(1783-1908), contains a box of vintage cabinet cards, daguerreotypes, engravings, silhouettes, and photographic postcards of Edwards family members.

Series IX, Artifacts
, includes a scrap of Sarah Pierpont Edwards's wedding dress and a linen bedsheet made by Esther Stoddard Edwards (box 36, folder 1656).

Series X, History of the Papers
(1767-1972) is organized into two sections: Correspondence, arranged by correspondent, and Agreements, Inventories, and Related Papers, arranged alphabetically by type. The correspondence consists of letters between descendants of Edwards, and regards the disposition of the papers.

odd.head: Appendix A: Guide to the Microfilm
odd.p: Series I, Writings of Jonathan Edwards , is housed in boxes 1-21 and is organized into three subseries: Sermons (boxes 1-15), Notebooks and Memoranda (boxes 15-21), and Personal Writings (box 21).

row.entry: First oversize target should be before folder 1, not after.

did.unittitle: Writings of Jonathan Edwards

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I, Writings of Jonathan Edwards , is organized in three subseries: Sermons; Notebooks and Memoranda; and Personal Writings. Sermons have been arranged by Biblical text, with sermon notebooks and unidentified fragments placed at the end of the subseries. Box 13 contains larger format sermons. Notebooks and Memoranda and Personal Writings are arranged alphabetically by title, subject, or type of material.

did.unittitle: PS. 112:1: "Good men are happy men"

descriptivenote.p: PS. 112:1: Good men are happy men

did.unittitle: PS. 115:1: "That it is the temper..."

descriptivenote.p: PS. 115:1: It is the spirit and temper of those that are truly godly, to delight to exalt God and to lay themselves low before him.

did.unittitle: PS. 119:1-6: "I. He that is a good..."

descriptivenote.p: PS. 119:1-6: I. He that is a good man walks in a holy way...

did.unittitle: PS. 119:18: "Men are naturally..."

descriptivenote.p: PS. 119:18: Men are naturally blind, so that they don't [see] the main things in the Word of God.

did.unittitle: PS. 119:60: "That we ought to make..."

descriptivenote.p: PS. 119:60: That we ought to make religion our present and immediate business.

did.unittitle: PS. 119:60: "Man is made to differ..."

descriptivenote.p: PS. 119:60: Man is made to differ from all the brute creatures in that respect, that he is capable of knowing God and knowing his will.

did.unittitle: PS. 119:60-61: "Men ought to be..."

descriptivenote.p: PS. 119:60-61: Men ought to be much in thinking of their own ways.

did.unittitle: PS. 145:15-21: "All creatures in..."

descriptivenote.p: PS. 145:15-21: All creatures in heaven and earth have their good things from God.

did.unittitle: PS. 147:1: "Its a very decent and..."

descriptivenote.p: PS. 147:1: It's a very decent and comely thing that praise should be given to God.

did.unittitle: PROV. 1:24-31: "Christ calls a..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 1:24-31: Christ calls a great deal to wicked men to forsake their sins and come to him and walk in his ways.

did.unittitle: PROV. 2:1-6: "1. Receive the words..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 2:1-6: 1. Receive the words of God . . . 2. Heed God's commandments or lay 'em up carefully . . . 3. Incline the ear . . .

did.unittitle: PROV. 8:10: "The grace of God in..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 8:10: The grace of God in the heart is better than silver and gold.

did.unittitle: PROV. 8:31: "Christ delighted in..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 8:31: Christ delighted in the thoughts of saving poor sinners before ever the world was made.

did.unittitle: PROV. 8:34-36: "1. What it is to..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 8:34-36: 1. What it is to hear Christ.

did.unittitle: PROV. 13:20: "If men will keep..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 13:20: If men will keep company with them that walk in wicked ways, nothing else is to be expected than that they will come to eternal destruction

did.unittitle: PROV. 18:10: "How Christ is a..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 18:10: How Christ is a strong tower.

did.unittitle: PROV. 19:8: "God in directing of us..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 19:8: God, in directing of us to seek after holiness, only advises us to be friends to our own souls.

did.unittitle: PROV. 24:13-14: "That it would be..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 24:13-14: That it would be worth the while to be religious if it were only for the pleasantness of it

did.unittitle: PROV. 28:1: "Righteousness tends to..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 28:1: Righteousness tends to make a man's heart bold and courageous.

did.unittitle: PROV. 29:25: "They are safe that..."

descriptivenote.p: PROV. 29:25: They are safe that trust in God.

did.unittitle: ECCL. 8:8: "I. Most men are very..."

descriptivenote.p: ECCL. 8:8: I. Most men are very much afraid of death. II. When death appears near, then especially are most men unwilling to die . . .

did.unittitle: ECCL. 8:11: "I. That God will..."

descriptivenote.p: ECCL. 8:11: I. That God will punish wicked men.

did.unittitle: ECCL. 9:10: "I. What [men] must do..."

descriptivenote.p: ECCL. 9:10: I. What [men] must do. Whatsoever thy hand finds to do. Everything that needs to be done in order to their salvation and in order to their being prepared for death.

did.unittitle: ECCL. 12:1: "By remembering [our...]"

descriptivenote.p: ECCL. 12:1: By remembering [our Creator] is meant that they should think much of him, and that they should seek him, and should give him that respect which is proper to give to him that made them.

did.unittitle: CANT. 1:3: "That Jesus Christ is..."

descriptivenote.p: CANT. 1:3: That Jesus Christ is a person transcendently excellent and lovely.

did.unittitle: IS. 3:10: "That a good man is a..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 3:10: That a good man is a happy man, whatever his outward condition is.

did.unittitle: IS. 6:3: "God is an infinitely holy..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 6:3(b): God is an infinitely holy [God].

did.unittitle: IS. 35:8: "Those only that are holy..."

descriptivenote.p: [Isaiah. 35:8: Those only that are holy...]

did.unittitle: IS. 52:15: "The suffering of Christ..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 52:15: The suffering of Christ for sinners is a very strange and wonderful thing.

did.unittitle: IS. 53:3: Text: "A man of sorrows..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 53:3(c): Text: A man of sorrows...

did.unittitle: IS. 53:7: "...suffered much from..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 53:7(b): . . . [Jesus] suffered much from his first coming into the world.

did.unittitle: IS. 55:1-3: "Obs. I. All mankind..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 55:1-3: Obs. I. All mankind seek after something to make 'em happy . . .

did.unittitle: IS. 55:6: "How men should seek and..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 55:6: How men should seek and call upon him.

did.unittitle: IS. 55:7-9: "The kindness of God is..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 55:7-9: The kindness of God is above all the kindness of men as the heavens are high above the earth.

did.unittitle: IS. 57:15: "I. God is the eternal..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 57:15: I. God is the eternal being. II. God is the high and lofty one. III. God is he whose name is holy . . .

did.unittitle: IS. 61:1: "Christ is come into the..."

descriptivenote.p: IS. 61:1: Christ is come into the world and brings good news for poor, distressed souls that are humbled and grieved for their sins.

did.unittitle: JER. 50:6: "Christ is as [it] were..."

descriptivenote.p: JER. 50:6: Christ is as [it] were the resting place of his people.

did.unittitle: EZEK. 7:16: "That at a time when a..."

descriptivenote.p: EZEK. 7:16: At a time when a people are called to a general humiliation, it becomes each one to mourn for his own iniquity. [1 of 2 folders]
EZEK. 7:16: At a time when a people are called to a general humiliation, it becomes each one to mourn for his own iniquity. [2 of 2 folders]

did.unittitle: EZEK. 18:30-32: "I. God will judge..."

descriptivenote.p: EZEK. 18:30-32: I. God will judge all men according to their ways.

did.unittitle: EZEK. 22:14: "I. When God comes to..."

descriptivenote.p: EZEK. 22:14: I. When God comes to punish wicked men for their sins, their hearts cannot endure it.

did.unittitle: EZEK. 33:7-9: "Tis kind and..."

descriptivenote.p: EZEK. 33:7-9: Tis kind and friendly in ministers, and not what they should at all dislike, when ministers warn sinners of their danger of God's wrath.

did.unittitle: HOS. 13:9: "Those sinners that are..."

descriptivenote.p: HOS. 13:9(b): Those sinners that are saved, it is God saves them.

did.unittitle: JOEL 2:12-13: "1. If men would..."

descriptivenote.p: JOEL 2:12-13: 1. If men would obtain mercy of God, they must mourn for sin.

did.unittitle: HAB. 1:13: "That [God] exceedingly..."

descriptivenote.p: HAB. 1:13: That [God] exceedingly hates sin.

did.unittitle: HAG. 1:5: "Tis the most important..."

descriptivenote.p: HAG. 1:5: Tis the most important duty of everyone to consider his own ways.

did.unittitle: ZECH. 4:7: "The gospel dispensation..."

descriptivenote.p: ZECH. 4:7: The gospel dispensation is finished wholly and entirely in free and glorious grace.

did.unittitle: MATT. 1:21: "...he shall save his..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 1:21: ... he shall save his people from their sins. I. From the punishment of sin ... II. Saves 'em from all the bad effects of sin ...

did.unittitle: MATT. 3:7-12: "I. Sin makes men's..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 3:7-12: I. Sin makes men's souls full of hatred to God and one to another as a viper is full of poison.

did.unittitle: MATT. 5:3: "Those persons that are..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 5:3: Those persons that are poor in spirit are happy and blessed in a right and title to the kingdom of heaven.

did.unittitle: MATT. 5:3: "1. What is meant by..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 5:3: 1. What is meant by being poor in spirit. 2. That such shall have the kingdom of heaven. 3. That they therefore are happy on that account.

did.unittitle: MATT. 5:4: "I. What the mourning is..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 5:4: I. What the mourning is here spoken [of]. Not the mourning of this world, but godly sorrow. II. Such mourning shall be comforted. III. Happy, more happy than those who have worldly comforts.

did.unittitle: MATT. 5:8: "1. What it is to be..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 5:8: 1. What it is to be pure in heart. 2. How the pure in heart shall see God. 3. That none but such shall see him. 4. That seeing God will make men truly happy.

did.unittitle: MATT. 6:19-20: "1. We should not be..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 6:19-20: 1. We should not be much concerned to lay up treasure on earth.

did.unittitle: MATT. 6:33: "By the kingdom of God..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 6:33(b): By 'the kingdom of God' in Scripture is meant here the great honor and happiness that is promised to the saints. . . . This should be sought first before other things . . .

did.unittitle: MATT. 7:13-14: "All mankind of all..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 7:13-14(b): All mankind of all nations, white and black, young and old, are going in one or the other of these paths, either in the way that leads to life or the way that leads to destruction.

did.unittitle: MATT. 7:14: "Tis a hard thing to..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 7:14(b): Tis a hard thing to find the right way to heaven.

did.unittitle: MATT. 7:24-27: "By the house which..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 7:24-27: By 'the house which the man builds' here is meant his hope of salvation. He that builds his house upon a rock, builds on a strong foundation.

did.unittitle: MATT. 10:14-15: "They that will not..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 10:14-15: They that will not harken to them that Christ sends to preach the gospel, it will be worse for 'em at the day of judgment than for the worst sort of them that never had the gospel offered to 'em....

did.unittitle: MATT. 10:37: "Doc. 1. If men don't..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 10:37: Doc. 1. If men don't love Christ better than anything else, Christ will not own 'em as those that belong to him.

did.unittitle: MATT. 11:28: "That Christ will..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 11:28: That Christ will freely give rest to all such as are spiritually weary and burdened that come to him for it ...

did.unittitle: MATT. 11:28: "1. What it is to come..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 11:28: 1. Matt. 11:28(b). 1. What it is to come to Christ . . . 2. Christ calls all that labor and are heavy laden . . . 3. Promises rest . . .

did.unittitle: MATT. 11:29-30: "I. How we should..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 11:29-30: I. How we should take Christ's yoke upon us . . . II. How we should learn of Christ.

did.unittitle: MATT. 12:42: "It would be worth the..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 12:42: It would be worth the while to go a great way to get acquaintance with Jesus Christ.

did.unittitle: MATT. 13:8: "Doc. 1. There are some..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 13:8: Doc. 1. There are some men that hear the word of God whose hearts are like good ground fitted to receive the seed sown. . . . II. Good men have such an understanding of the word of God as wicked men never have.

did.unittitle: MATT. 13:37-43: "I. Good men are..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 13:37-43: I. Good men are like good wheat which Christ sows in his field. II. God is the Father of good men. III. Wicked men are the children of the devil. IV. Wicked men that live among Christians are like tares among the wheat in a field. V. The e

did.unittitle: MATT. 13:40-43: "1. This world will..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 13:40-43: 1. This world will come to an end.

did.unittitle: MATT. 13:42: "Subject: the misery..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 13:42: Subject: the misery of wicked men in another world.

did.unittitle: MATT. 13:47-50: "So none ought to..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 13:47-50(i): So none ought to come into the Christian church but good men. But because ministers can't know men's hearts, every sort will come in, good and bad. As there will be some bad fish in the net, so there will be some bad men in the kingdom

did.unittitle: MATT. 16:26: "That the salvation of..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 16:26: The salvation of the soul is a thing of vastly more worth and importance than the whole world. [1 of 2 folders]
MATT. 16:26: The salvation of the soul is a thing of vastly more worth and importance than the whole world. [2 of 2 folders]

did.unittitle: MATT. 17:5: "I. Christ is the Son..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 17:5: I. Christ is the Son of God. . . . Christ is both God and man. . . . So he was the Son of God from all eternity.

did.unittitle: MATT. 21:5: "The external meanness..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 21:5: The external meanness of Christ's appearance in the world is a thing worthy of the highest admiration.

did.unittitle: MATT. 22:1-14: "Obs. 1. The church..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 22:1-14: Obs. 1. The church of Christ is as it were married to Christ.

did.unittitle: MATT. 22:37-38: "1. What it is to..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 22:37-38: 1. What it is to love God. 2. The reason why we ought to love him. 3. Why [we ought to love him] with all our heart. 4. How this is the greatest of all the commandments.

did.unittitle: MATT. 22:39: "That we should love..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 22:39: That we should love all men, but 'tis not meant that we should love all alike. Love some more than others, because they deserve more love.

did.unittitle: MATT. 23:37: [Christ] is ready to..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 23:37: [Christ] is ready to receive sinners and love 'em and show great mercy to 'em, if they will come to him.

did.unittitle: MATT. 25:1-13: "Obs. 1. Christ is..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 25:1-13: Obs. 1. Christ is the bridegroom of the church. 2. Professors of religion in general hope to go to heaven. 3. Many of the professors are not true Christians ...

did.unittitle: MATT. 28:6-7: "1. How he rose..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 28:6-7: 1. How he rose . . . 2. Evidence of his resurrection . . . 3. Why it was necessary.

did.unittitle: MATT. 28:19-20: "4. They that are..."

descriptivenote.p: MATT. 28:19-20: 4. They that are baptized are under great obligations to observe all the commands of Christ.

did.unittitle: MARK 10:17-27: "Obs. 1. There are..."

descriptivenote.p: MARK 10:17-27: Obs. 1. There are many that have a great desire to have eternal life and take considerable pains for it, that yet never obtain it.

did.unittitle: MARK 16:15-16: "What is meant by ..."

descriptivenote.p: MARK 16:15-16: What is meant by believing in Christ. For a man to believe in Christ is to come to him with all the heart, to take him for his Savior and give himself to him, to be one of his people, and to have all his dependence on Christ to make him hap

did.unittitle: LUKE 1:77-79: "I. God's people have..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 1:77-79: I. God's people have the forgiveness of their sins by Jesus Christ.

did.unittitle: LUKE 2:6-14: "I. What was that was..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 2:6-14: I. What was that was born of the Virgin . . . II. Whom he was born for . . . III. What need there was of his being born and being man.

did.unittitle: LUKE 2:10-11: "The word of God..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 2:10-11: The Word of God, which tells us about Jesus Christ, is good news.

did.unittitle: LUKE 2:14: "That the Son of God by..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 2:14(a): That the Son of God, by appearing in our nature, laid a glorious foundation for peace to the inhabitants of this world.

did.unittitle: LUKE 2:14: "That Christ's appearing..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 2:14: That Christ's appearing in our nature is an instance of wonderful love to man.

did.unittitle: LUKE 4:18: "I. Shew you how the..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 4:18: I. Show you how the Spirit of God was given to Christ.

did.unittitle: LUKE 5:5-6: "That it is our best..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 5:5-6: That it is our best way in all cases, to follow the directions that Christ gives us.

did.unittitle: LUKE 6:45: "The speech of their..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 6:45(a): The speech of their mouths will show that which their heart is full of.

did.unittitle: LUKE 6:45: "I. A wicked man has a..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 6:45(c): I. A wicked man has a treasure of evil in his heart ...

did.unittitle: LUKE 8:26-39: "II. How wicked men..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 8:26-39: II. How wicked men are like the man possessed with devils.

did.unittitle: LUKE 9:23: "That if we would be..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 9:23(a): That if we would be Christ's disciples, it is necessary that we should deny ourselves.

did.unittitle: LUKE 9:23: "I. Why it is necessary..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 9:23(b): I. Why it is necessary for a man, if he would be a true Christian, [that he] must deny himself.

did.unittitle: LUKE 9:56: "Christ came into the..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 9:56: Christ came into the world to save men's lives.

did.unittitle: LUKE 11:24-26: "I. The devil..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 11:24-26: I. The devil naturally dwells in the hearts of men.

did.unittitle: LUKE 11:27-28: "That the hearing..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 11:27-28: That the hearing and keeping the word of God renders a person more blessed than any other privilege that ever God bestowed on any of the children of men

did.unittitle: LUKE 13:5: "Without true repentance..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 13:5: Without true repentance for sin, there is no escaping eternal ruin

did.unittitle: LUKE 13:7: "A people that live..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 13:7(b): A people that live under the gospel are as it were God's orchard

did.unittitle: LUKE 14:16: "Obs. 1. The provision..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 14:16: Obs. 1. The provision which God has made for the good for the souls of men by Jesus Christ is like a great feast

did.unittitle: LUKE 15:3-7: "Christ is to his..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 15:3-7: Christ is to his people as a shepherd to his flock

did.unittitle: LUKE 15:11-24: "I. God is the..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 15:11-24: I. God is the Father of mankind . . . II. Wicked men are not willing to serve God, but have a mind to be for themselves and not for God

did.unittitle: LUKE 16:19: "1. God is as willing..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 16:19: 1. God is as willing to show mercy to one nation as another, if they repent to turn to him

did.unittitle: LUKE 16:22-23: "That which I intend..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 16:22-23(b): That which I intend to speak of at this time from this text, is the punishment of wicked men in hell after death

did.unittitle: LUKE 16:27-28: "When [men] come..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 16:27-28: When [men] come into another world, they will have their eyes opened and will see better than they do now the things they had been taught in this world

did.unittitle: LUKE 18:1: "I. What is prayer..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 18:1: I. What is prayer. II. All men ought to pray to God . . .III. Pray always . . . IV. What we should pray for

did.unittitle: LUKE 18:13: "Now therefore you, if..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 18:13: Now therefore you, if ever you obtain the favor of God, must do it as this man [the publican] did

did.unittitle: LUKE 19:10: "What is meant is that..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 19:10(b): What is meant is that he [the Son of man] came into the world to seek and save sinners

did.unittitle: LUKE 21:36: "I. Many dreadful..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 21:36: I. Many dreadful things are coming upon this world. II. The righteous, and they only, shall be thought fit to escape those things that shall come. III. The righteous shall be called to appear before Christ. . . . IV. The righteous shall be tho

did.unittitle: LUKE 24:47: "In speaking from this..."

descriptivenote.p: LUKE 24:47: In speaking from this text I would, 1. Show you what true repentance is. 2. How they have their sins forgiven 'em who have this repentance. 3. How this forgiveness of sin is through Christ. 4. That this forgiveness is offered to all nations if

did.unittitle: JOHN 1:12: "Good men whose hearts..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 1:12: Good men whose hearts receive Jesus Christ are God's children

did.unittitle: JOHN 1:12-13: "I. How true..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 1:12-13: I. How true believers receive Christ . . . II. How God gives such as receive Christ the privileges of his children

did.unittitle: JOHN 1:29: "1. How that Christ is..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 1:29: 1. How that Christ is like a lamb. 2. [That he] is the Lamb that takes away the sin of the world. 3. [That] we should look upon and behold this Lamb of God

did.unittitle: JOHN 1:46: "Should come to Christ..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 1:46: [You] should come to Christ and give your hearts to Christ

did.unittitle: JOHN 6:68: "It is by Christ alone..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 6:68: It is by Christ alone that eternal life is ever communicated to men

did.unittitle: JOHN 7:34: "Some men seek salvation..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 7:34: Some men seek salvation and never obtain it

did.unittitle: JOHN 8:12: "That Jesus Christ is..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 8:12(a): That Jesus Christ is the light of the world. We propose thus to handle this doctrine: 1. To show how the world is in darkness without Christ 2. How it is enlightened by him. 3. To show by what means Christ communicates his light

did.unittitle: JOHN 8:34: "That wicked men are..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 8:34: That wicked men are servants and slaves to sin

did.unittitle: JOHN 10:27: "Hear Christ's voice..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 10:27: Hear Christ's voice. Understand. Believe. Obey

did.unittitle: JOHN 15:5: "That Christ Jesus is..."

descriptivenote.p: JOHN 15:5(a): That Christ Jesus is the original and fountain of all spiritual life and nourishment

did.unittitle: ACTS 2:37: "Commonly sinners before..."

descriptivenote.p: ACTS 2:37: Commonly sinners, before they are converted and have their sins forgiven them, are pricked and wounded in their heart

did.unittitle: ACTS 3:22-23: "I. Christ is a great..."

descriptivenote.p: ACTS 3:22-23: I. Christ is a great prophet. II. Made our teacher. III. Ought to hear him. IV. Ought to hear whatever . . . V. Everyone that will not hear, God will destroy from among his people

did.unittitle: ACTS 11:12-13: "Now I am come to..."

descriptivenote.p: ACTS 11:12-13: Now I am come to...

did.unittitle: ACTS 14:26-27: "Tis a special and..."

descriptivenote.p: ACTS 14:26-27: Tis a special and glorious work of God to open the way for the propagation of the gospel among the heathen

did.unittitle: ACTS 16:9: "This may teach you how ..."

descriptivenote.p: ACTS 16:9: This may teach you how great your calamity was before you had the gospel preached, and what cause of thankfulness

did.unittitle: ACTS 16:29-31: "I. They that are..."

descriptivenote.p: ACTS 16:29-31(b): I. They that are not converted are in a dreadful condition. II. That is a thing that men should be greatly concerned about, to know what they shall do to be saved

did.unittitle: ACTS 16:30: "Tis of exceeding great..."

descriptivenote.p: ACTS 16:30: Tis of exceeding great importance, and what men had need to be greatly concerned about, that they should be saved

did.unittitle: ROM. 1:21: "The subject. The great..."

descriptivenote.p: ROM. 1:21: The subject: the great obligation we are under to be thankful to God for his goodness

did.unittitle: ROM. 1:24: "God sometimes punishes..."

descriptivenote.p: ROM. 1:24: God sometimes punishes sin by giving men up to sin

did.unittitle: ROM. 8:34: "Tis impossible that..."

descriptivenote.p: ROM. 8:34: Tis impossible that they that believe in Christ should perish, because he died and rose again and went to heaven and lives in heaven for their salvation

did.unittitle: ROM. 12:1: "Tis our most reasonable..."

descriptivenote.p: ROM. 12:1(a): Tis our most reasonable duty to offer and give ourselves up to God

did.unittitle: ROM. 12:12: "I. Tis all men's duty..."

descriptivenote.p: ROM. 12:12: I. Tis all men's duty to pray. II. Should be earnest in prayer. III. Should continue in earnest prayer to the end

did.unittitle: ROM. 12:18: "It is an indispensable..."

descriptivenote.p: ROM. 12:18: It is an indispensable duty incumbent upon us, to endeavor to the utmost of our power to live peaceably with all men

did.unittitle: I COR. 6:19-20: "1. How Christians..."

descriptivenote.p: I COR. 6:19-20(b): 1. How Christians are bought with a price. . . . 2. The price he bought them with was his own blood. 3. What Christ has bought 'em for

did.unittitle: I COR. 6:19-20: "I. Christians are..."

descriptivenote.p: I COR. 6:19-20(c): I. Christians are bought with a price . . . II. Therefore we are God's . . . III. Therefore they are not their own . . . IV. Therefore we should glorify God in our bodies and in our spirits

did.unittitle: I COR. 10:17: "The Lord's Supper..."

descriptivenote.p: I COR. 10:17(b): The Lord's Supper was instituted as a solemn representation and seal of the holy and spiritual union of Christ's people with Christ and one to another

did.unittitle: I COR. 11:23-25: "I. What is meant..."

descriptivenote.p: I COR. 11:23-25: I. What is meant by the bread being Christ's body and the wine his blood. . . . II. What is meant by Christ's body being broken for us. . . .

did.unittitle: I COR. 11:27: "Here, 1. I will tell..."

descriptivenote.p: I COR. 11:27: Here, 1. I will tell you who they be that eat [and drink] unworthily. 2. Why such as [eat and drink] unworthily are guilty of murdering the body and shedding the blood of Christ

did.unittitle: I COR. 11:28: "[Text: But leave a..."

descriptivenote.p: I COR. 11:28: [Text: 'But leave a man examine himself and so . . . eat.'] 1. Show how. 2. Give the reason why

did.unittitle: II COR. 4:3-4: "I. How the gospel..."

descriptivenote.p: II COR. 4:3-4: I. How the gospel is hid from them that continue in sin under the gospel

did.unittitle: II COR. 4:7: "That God is pleased..."

descriptivenote.p: II COR. 4:7: That God is pleased to make his own power appear by carrying on the work of his grace by such instruments as men, that in themselves are utterly insufficient for it

did.unittitle: II COR. 4:18: "But there is another..."

descriptivenote.p: II COR. 4:18(d): But there is another world that now is not seen, and the things of that have no end . .

did.unittitle: II COR. 5:17: "1. What is meant by..."

descriptivenote.p: II COR. 5:17: 1. What is meant by being in Christ. 2. How he that is in Christ is a new creature. 3. How old things are passed away in him that is in Christ, and all things are become new

did.unittitle: EPH. 4:22-24: "I. Men naturally are..."

descriptivenote.p: EPH. 4:22-24: I. Men naturally are corrupt and live according to their wicked inclinations . . . II. The lusts of men's hearts are deceitful . . . III. They that are converted and made good men, put off the old man and are made new men

did.unittitle: PHIL. 1:21: "Everyone that is a..."

descriptivenote.p: PHIL. 1:21(a): Everyone that is a true Christian lives to Christ

did.unittitle: PHIL. 1:21: "Death is gainful to..."

descriptivenote.p: PHIL. 1:21(b): Death is gainful to the true Christian

did.unittitle: PHIL. 2:7: "[The incarnation and..."

descriptivenote.p: PHIL. 2:7: [The incarnation and humiliation of Christ] show you how Christ became a man

did.unittitle: PHIL. 2:8: "Our Lord Jesus Christ..."

descriptivenote.p: PHIL. 2:8: Our Lord Jesus Christ suffered death

did.unittitle: COL. 1:12: "If men would go to..."

descriptivenote.p: COL. 1:12: If men would go to heaven, they must first be made fit for it

did.unittitle: I THESS. 5:5: "Shew how true..."

descriptivenote.p: I THESS. 5:5(b): Show how true Christians are children of light and of the day

did.unittitle: II THESS. 2:13: "He whose work it..."

descriptivenote.p: II THESS. 2:13: He whose work it is to make men holy is the Holy Spirit

did.unittitle: II THESS. 3:10-13: "...the sin of..."

descriptivenote.p: II THESS. 3:10-13: Subject: the sin of idleness, or men's spending their time without diligently following any business

did.unittitle: I TIM. 3:16: "Jesus Christ is both..."

descriptivenote.p: I TIM. 3:16: Jesus Christ is both God and man

did.unittitle: TITUS 1:2: "God hath made provision..."

descriptivenote.p: TITUS 1:2: God hath made provision that some Christians should have good reason that true Christians should hope for eternal life

did.unittitle: HEB. 3:7-11: "They that live under..."

descriptivenote.p: HEB. 3:7-11: They that live under the gospel should take care that it ben't with them as it was with the wicked Israelites of old that God destroyed in the wilderness

did.unittitle: HEB. 9:12: "Jesus Christ is both..."

descriptivenote.p: HEB. 9:12: Jesus Christ is both the only priest and sacrifice by which eternal redemption is obtained for believers

did.unittitle: HEB. 9:27: "All men must certainly..."

descriptivenote.p: HEB. 9:27: All men must certainly die, and after death their everlasting state will be determined [1 of 2 folders]
HEB. 9:27: All men must certainly die, and after death their everlasting state will be determined [2 of 2 folders]

did.unittitle: HEB. 9:27: "All men of all nations..."

descriptivenote.p: HEB. 9:27(b): All men, of all nations, all over the world, must die

did.unittitle: HEB. 11:16: "I. This world is an..."

descriptivenote.p: HEB. 11:16(b): I. This world is an evil country . . .. II. Heaven is a better country

did.unittitle: HEB. 13:7-8: ["Farewell sermon..."

descriptivenote.p: HEB. 13:7-8: Farewell sermon to Indians in Stockbridge

did.unittitle: JAMES 1:12 "The doctrine was, that..."

descriptivenote.p: JAMES 1:12(a): . . . the doctrine was, that those who love Christ shall receive of him a crown of life

did.unittitle: JAMES 1:25: "In God's service is..."

descriptivenote.p: JAMES 1:25: In God's service is perfect and glorious liberty

did.unittitle: I PET. 1:8: "Three things: loving..."

descriptivenote.p: I PET. 1:8(c): Three things: loving Christ, believing in him, rejoicing in him

did.unittitle: I PET. 1:15: "That Christians are..."

descriptivenote.p: I PET. 1:15: That Christians are under special obligations to be universally holy in their lives [1 of 2 folders]
I PET. 1:15: That Christians are under special obligations to be universally holy in their lives [2 of 2 folders]

did.unittitle: I PET. 4:7: "It becomes [us] to be..."

descriptivenote.p: I PET. 4:7: It becomes [us] to be sober and serious and make religion our great business for that reason, that all the things of this world will soon come to an end

did.unittitle: II PET. 2:4: "The fall of the..."

descriptivenote.p: II PET. 2:4: Subject: the fall of the angels that sinned and afterwards became devils

did.unittitle: I JOHN 3:10: "All mankind through..."

descriptivenote.p: I JOHN 3:10: All mankind through the whole world are of one of these two sorts, either God's people or the devil's people

did.unittitle: I JOHN 5:3: "The true love to God..."

descriptivenote.p: I JOHN 5:3: True love to God makes the duties he requires of us easy and delightful

did.unittitle: JUDE 13: "The misery of the wicked..."

descriptivenote.p: JUDE 13(b): The misery of the wicked in another world will be as it were everlasting darkness without any light

did.unittitle: REV. 1:7: "...this coming of Christ..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 1:7: . . . this coming of Christ at the day of judgment is what I shall make the subject of my present discourse

did.unittitle: REV. 3:20: "The Lord Jesus Christ..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 3:20(c): The Lord Jesus Christ by coming into the world and dying for sinners, has obtained salvation for all such as will repent and turn from their sins and give their hearts to him

did.unittitle: REV. 6:13-17: "I. Tho' now many..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 6:13-17: I. Tho' now many

did.unittitle: REV. 14:4: "The saints are those..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 14:4: The saints are those that God has chosen out of all the world to be for him as his part and portion

did.unittitle: REV. 14:18-19: "That God sometimes..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 14:18-19: That God sometimes defers the punishment of sinners till they are fully ripe for destruction

did.unittitle: REV. 19:1: "The work and business..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 19:1: The work and business of saints and angels in heaven consist very much in praising God

did.unittitle: REV. 21:18: "That there is nothing..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 21:18(a): That there is nothing upon earth that will suffice to represent to us the glories of heaven [1 of 2 folders]
REV. 21:18(a): That there is nothing upon earth that will suffice to represent to us the glories of heaven [2 of 2 folders]

did.unittitle: REV. 22:3-5: Text: "And there shall..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 22:3-5: Text: And there shall

did.unittitle: REV. 22:5: "The saints in heaven..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 22:5: The saints in heaven shall have light without any darkness

did.unittitle: REV. 22:11-12: "The time will come..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 22:11-12: The time will come that as men are at that time, whether good or bad, so they will be to all eternity; they will never change

did.unittitle: REV. 22:12: "Christ will quickly..."

descriptivenote.p: REV. 22:12: Christ will quickly come to judge men for what they do in the world

did.unittitle: Edwards Family Correspondence

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series V, Edwards Family Correspondence , is organized in four subseries: Jonathan Edwards, 1745-1801; Jonathan Walter Edwards; Hannah Edwards Wetmore; and Other Correspondents. Letters are arranged alphabetically by correspondent and, within the first three subseries, are subdivided into Outgoing Letters and Incoming Letters.
